Let me read the rest of it:', 'type': 'text'}, {'id': 'toolu_01S6Rv2eNNauXr1BfmE7PHmP', 'caller': {'type': 'direct'}, 'input': {'limit': 300, 'offset': 200, 'path': 'piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/openpiton/riscv_peripherals.sv'}, 'name': 'read_file', 'type': 'tool_use'}]

[{'text': 'Now let me search for specific vulnerability patterns:', 'type': 'text'}, {'id': 'toolu_018cBzduApzYnU2h8SgKFo7z', 'caller': {'type': 'direct'}, 'input': {'path': '.', 'query': 'rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock'}, 'name': 'search_text', 'type': 'tool_use'}, {'id': 'toolu_015xswVgwHFqaYfkbTHjkbUs', 'caller': {'type': 'direct'}, 'input': {'path': '.', 'query': 'reglk_mem[2]'}, 'name': 'search_text', 'type': 'tool_use'}]

{"analysis_summary": "Analysis of the RTL source files under the HackAtDAC21 `h21_access_reglock_reset_expansion_scope` dataset revealed multiple confirmed and potential permission-related security vulnerabilities in the register-lock (REGLK), access-control-table (ACCT), and reset-controller (RST_CTRL) modules. The vulnerabilities fall into four categories: (1) incorrect register-lock bit used for `reglk_mem[0]` write protection, (2) a wrong-register data assignment bug in `reglk_mem[2]` write path, (3) inconsistent/mismatched read vs. write lock bits in `acct_wrapper`, and (4) mismatched/out-of-bounds lock-bit indices in `acct_wrapper` write path (using `reglk_ctrl[13]` on an 8-bit input). Additionally, a boot-mode selection logic bug was found where `ariane_boot_sel_i` is ignored and both branches always return `rom_rdata_linux`. The reset controller module is also missing the `jtag_unlock` reset expansion that is present in the REGLK module, which means a JTAG-triggered unlock does not reset the RST_CTRL peripheral state.", "findings": [{"finding_id": "FIND-01", "status": "confirmed_finding", "summary": "reglk_mem[0] write-lock uses wrong bit: reglk_ctrl[3] instead of reglk_ctrl[1]", "vulnerability_category": "Register Lock Bypass / Incorrect Access Control", "affected_locations": [{"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/reglk/reglk_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 89, "line_end": 89, "module": "reglk_wrapper", "signal_or_register": "reglk_mem[0]"}], "evidence": [{"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/reglk/reglk_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 89, "line_end": 89, "module": "", "object": "reglk_mem[0] write case", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "Case 0 uses reglk_ctrl[3] as write-lock: `reglk_mem[0] <= reglk_ctrl[3] ? reglk_mem[0] : wdata;`", "supports_claim": "All other reglk_mem entries (1–5) use reglk_ctrl[1] as the write-lock bit. reglk_mem[0] inconsistently uses reglk_ctrl[3], meaning if bit 3 happens to be clear when bit 1 is set (or vice versa), the lock behaviour differs from the intended policy."}, {"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/reglk/reglk_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 91, "line_end": 99, "module": "", "object": "reglk_mem[1..5] write cases", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "All other entries use reglk_ctrl[1]: `reglk_mem[1] <= reglk_ctrl[1] ? reglk_mem[1] : wdata;`", "supports_claim": "The divergence in lock-bit selection for reglk_mem[0] vs all others is a design inconsistency strongly suggesting an introduced fault."}], "reasoning_summary": "The write side of reglk_wrapper uses reglk_ctrl[1] to protect writes to reglk_mem[1] through reglk_mem[5], but reglk_mem[0] is protected by reglk_ctrl[3]. This means the write-lock policy for the first entry of the register-lock memory does not follow the same protection bit as the rest of the entries. An attacker who can write reglk_ctrl[3]=0 while reglk_ctrl[1]=1 (lock set for others) can freely overwrite reglk_mem[0], which controls lock outputs for peripheral 0 (e.g. ROM). Conversely, if reglk_ctrl[3]=1 but reglk_ctrl[1]=0, reglk_mem[0] is locked while others can be freely overwritten.", "security_impact": "Incorrect lock-bit assignment for reglk_mem[0] allows unauthorized modification of the register-lock value for peripheral 0 (Boot ROM / first peripheral), potentially enabling an attacker to override security lock settings that protect sensitive peripherals from software access.", "confidence": "high", "uncertainty_or_missing_evidence": "It is not externally visible which bit index is architecturally correct; however, the inconsistency with all sibling entries strongly implies this is a bug/vulnerability introduced intentionally in the HackAtDAC dataset.", "recommended_follow_up": ["Verify intended lock-bit assignment for reglk_mem[0] against the hardware security architecture specification.", "Change reglk_ctrl[3] to reglk_ctrl[1] for reglk_mem[0] write protection if uniform policy is intended."]}, {"finding_id": "FIND-02", "status": "confirmed_finding", "summary": "reglk_mem[2] write case assigns reglk_mem[3] (wrong source) instead of preserving reglk_mem[2] when locked", "vulnerability_category": "Register Lock Bypass / Data Integrity Fault", "affected_locations": [{"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/reglk/reglk_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 93, "line_end": 93, "module": "reglk_wrapper", "signal_or_register": "reglk_mem[2]"}], "evidence": [{"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/reglk/reglk_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 93, "line_end": 93, "module": "", "object": "reglk_mem[2] write case", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "`reglk_mem[2] <= reglk_ctrl[1] ? reglk_mem[3] : wdata;` — when locked (reglk_ctrl[1]=1), the register is loaded with reglk_mem[3] instead of reglk_mem[2].", "supports_claim": "Every other entry (1, 3, 4, 5) uses the self-referential form `reglk_mem[X] <= reglk_ctrl[1] ? reglk_mem[X] : wdata`, preserving the current value when locked. reglk_mem[2] wrongly uses reglk_mem[3] as the 'hold' value."}, {"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/reglk/reglk_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 95, "line_end": 99, "module": "", "object": "reglk_mem[3..5] write cases", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "`reglk_mem[3] <= reglk_ctrl[1] ? reglk_mem[3] : wdata;` etc. — correct self-referential hold.", "supports_claim": "Confirms reglk_mem[2] is the only entry with a cross-register reference, making it a targeted fault."}], "reasoning_summary": "When reglk_ctrl[1]=1 (lock active) and a write to address 2 occurs, reglk_mem[2] gets overwritten with the current value of reglk_mem[3] rather than retaining its own value. This allows an adversary who can cause a spurious (or maliciously-triggered) write cycle to index 2 while the lock is set to silently corrupt the register-lock configuration for peripheral 2 with the value intended for peripheral 3. This undermines the integrity of the lock mechanism.", "security_impact": "An attacker who can generate a write transaction to reglk_mem[2] while the lock bit is set can corrupt the lock register for peripheral 2 (AES1 / SHA256 lock bytes) by copying peripheral 3's lock value into it. This can either loosen or tighten access control on peripheral 2 in an unintended way, enabling unauthorized access to crypto peripherals.", "confidence": "high", "uncertainty_or_missing_evidence": "The exact peripheral mapping depends on the full reglk_ctrl_o bit-field assignment, which is partially visible; the impact is still clearly a data integrity violation in the lock register.", "recommended_follow_up": ["Fix the locked hold-value source from reglk_mem[3] to reglk_mem[2] in case 2 of the write always block."]}, {"finding_id": "FIND-03", "status": "confirmed_finding", "summary": "acct_wrapper write-lock for acct_mem[3..5] uses reglk_ctrl[13], which is out-of-range for an 8-bit reglk_ctrl_i input", "vulnerability_category": "Register Lock Bypass / Out-of-Range Bit Index", "affected_locations": [{"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/acct/acct_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 96, "line_end": 100, "module": "acct_wrapper", "signal_or_register": "acct_mem[03], acct_mem[04], acct_mem[05]"}], "evidence": [{"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/acct/acct_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 19, "line_end": 19, "module": "", "object": "reglk_ctrl_i port declaration", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "`input logic [7:0] reglk_ctrl_i;` — the input is only 8 bits wide (bits 7:0).", "supports_claim": "Any bit index ≥ 8 is zero-padded or results in unintended behaviour depending on synthesis tool."}, {"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/acct/acct_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 96, "line_end": 100, "module": "", "object": "acct_mem[03..05] write cases", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "`acct_mem[03] <= reglk_ctrl[13] ? acct_mem[03] : wdata;` uses bit index 13 of a 16-bit internal reglk_ctrl signal, which is derived from an 8-bit input assigned to a 16-bit wire.", "supports_claim": "reglk_ctrl is declared as `logic [15:0]` and assigned from the 8-bit `reglk_ctrl_i`, so bits [15:8] are always 0. reglk_ctrl[13] is therefore always 0, meaning the lock on acct_mem[03..05] is permanently disabled."}, {"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/acct/acct_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 43, "line_end": 43, "module": "", "object": "reglk_ctrl assignment", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "`assign reglk_ctrl = reglk_ctrl_i;` — 8-bit value sign/zero-extended into 16-bit wire, upper 8 bits always 0.", "supports_claim": "Confirms that bits [15:8] of reglk_ctrl are always 0."}], "reasoning_summary": "The acct_wrapper declares reglk_ctrl_i as 8 bits wide. Internally it is widened to a 16-bit wire reglk_ctrl. The write-lock for acct_mem[03], [04], and [05] (which appear to be HMAC access-control entries) checks reglk_ctrl[13], which is always 0 because the input only provides 8 bits. As a result, the lock for those three entries is permanently bypassed — writes are always accepted regardless of the intended register-lock state.", "security_impact": "acct_mem[03], [04], and [05] — the access-control table entries for HMAC (peripheral 4) — can always be overwritten by any software that can reach the ACCT peripheral, even when the system intends those entries to be locked. This undermines the hardware-enforced access-control policy for cryptographic peripherals.", "confidence": "high", "uncertainty_or_missing_evidence": "The exact peripheral mapping for acct_mem indices 3–5 is inferred from context (HMAC is peripheral index 4 based on rst_wrapper). Full peripheral index table is not in scope.", "recommended_follow_up": ["Replace reglk_ctrl[13] with the correct in-range bit index (likely reglk_ctrl[3] or reglk_ctrl[5]) for acct_mem[03..05] write protection.", "Increase reglk_ctrl_i port width if bits above 7 are genuinely needed."]}, {"finding_id": "FIND-04", "status": "confirmed_finding", "summary": "acct_wrapper read-lock and write-lock bits are mismatched for acct_mem[0..8]: different bits protect read vs. write", "vulnerability_category": "Inconsistent Access Control Policy", "affected_locations": [{"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/acct/acct_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 88, "line_end": 140, "module": "acct_wrapper", "signal_or_register": "acct_mem[00..08]"}], "evidence": [{"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/acct/acct_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 90, "line_end": 94, "module": "", "object": "Write lock for acct_mem[00..02]", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "Write protected by reglk_ctrl[5]: `acct_mem[00] <= reglk_ctrl[5] ? acct_mem[00] : wdata;`", "supports_claim": "Write uses bit 5."}, {"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/acct/acct_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 121, "line_end": 125, "module": "", "object": "Read lock for acct_mem[00..02]", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "Read gated by reglk_ctrl[4]: `rdata = reglk_ctrl[4] ? 'b0 : acct_mem[0];`", "supports_claim": "Read uses bit 4, not bit 5. The read and write locks are controlled by different bits."}, {"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/acct/acct_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 96, "line_end": 100, "module": "", "object": "Write lock for acct_mem[03..05]", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "Write gated by reglk_ctrl[13] (always 0, see FIND-03): `acct_mem[03] <= reglk_ctrl[13] ? acct_mem[03] : wdata;`", "supports_claim": "Write uses (broken) bit 13."}, {"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/acct/acct_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 127, "line_end": 131, "module": "", "object": "Read lock for acct_mem[03..05]", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "Read gated by reglk_ctrl[2]: `rdata = reglk_ctrl[2] ? 'b0 : acct_mem[3];`", "supports_claim": "Read uses bit 2, write uses bit 13 (always 0). Completely independent bits."}, {"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/acct/acct_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 102, "line_end": 106, "module": "", "object": "Write lock for acct_mem[06..08]", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "Write gated by reglk_ctrl[1].", "supports_claim": "Write uses bit 1."}, {"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/acct/acct_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 133, "line_end": 137, "module": "", "object": "Read lock for acct_mem[06..08]", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "Read gated by reglk_ctrl[0].", "supports_claim": "Read uses bit 0, write uses bit 1. Mismatched."}], "reasoning_summary": "For every group of acct_mem entries, the read-lock bit and the write-lock bit are different register-lock control bits. This creates a situation where an attacker could set the lock to prevent reads (confidentiality) but the write-lock comes from a different bit which may still be clear — or vice versa. The mismatch makes the access-control policy incoherent and potentially exploitable: an adversary who can manipulate individual reglk_ctrl bits can decouple read and write protection in unexpected ways.", "security_impact": "Incoherent read/write locking of the access-control table entries allows selective bypass of either read or write protection for each group of ACCT entries. This can lead to information leakage (reads unprotected when write lock is set) or unauthorized modification of access-control policies.", "confidence": "high", "uncertainty_or_missing_evidence": "It is theoretically possible that the design intentionally uses different bits for read and write locks (i.e., separate read-enable and write-enable lock controls), but this is unusual and contrary to normal secure design practice; no documentation in scope supports an intentional design.", "recommended_follow_up": ["Audit the intended lock-bit mapping for the ACCT module against the security architecture.", "Ensure the read and write lock for each acct_mem group use the same control bit."]}, {"finding_id": "FIND-05", "status": "confirmed_finding", "summary": "reglk_wrapper reset condition includes jtag_unlock, allowing JTAG to clear all register-lock values; rst_ctrl and acct_wrapper do not have equivalent jtag_unlock reset expansion", "vulnerability_category": "Unintended Reset / Privilege Escalation via JTAG", "affected_locations": [{"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/reglk/reglk_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 80, "line_end": 83, "module": "reglk_wrapper", "signal_or_register": "reglk_mem"}, {"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/acct/acct_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 81, "line_end": 84, "module": "acct_wrapper", "signal_or_register": "acct_mem"}, {"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/rst_ctrl/rst_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 73, "line_end": 76, "module": "rst_wrapper", "signal_or_register": "rst_mem, start, rst_id"}], "evidence": [{"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/reglk/reglk_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 80, "line_end": 83, "module": "", "object": "reglk_wrapper reset condition", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "`if(~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9))` — reset clears all reglk_mem entries when jtag_unlock is asserted.", "supports_claim": "JTAG unlock explicitly triggers a reset of all register-lock values, clearing them to 0 (unlocked state). This is a deliberate design: JTAG can override register locks."}, {"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/acct/acct_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 81, "line_end": 84, "module": "", "object": "acct_wrapper reset condition", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "`if(~(rst_ni && ~rst_6))` — reset initializes acct_mem to 0xFFFFFFFF (fully permissive). No jtag_unlock input present.", "supports_claim": "The ACCT module does NOT have jtag_unlock in its port list or reset condition. If JTAG unlocks reglk_wrapper (clearing lock bits), the ACCT module's acct_mem is not reset, creating an inconsistent security state."}, {"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/rst_ctrl/rst_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 73, "line_end": 76, "module": "", "object": "rst_wrapper reset condition", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "`if (~rst_ni || rst_12)` — no jtag_unlock in reset condition or port list.", "supports_claim": "The RST_CTRL module does NOT reset on jtag_unlock. Its rst_mem is not cleared when JTAG asserts an unlock, leaving the reset-controller state potentially inconsistent with the cleared REGLK state."}], "reasoning_summary": "The reglk_wrapper module includes jtag_unlock in its reset condition, meaning asserting the JTAG unlock signal clears all lock registers. This creates a powerful privilege-escalation path: any entity capable of asserting jtag_unlock can zero out all peripheral lock registers. Meanwhile, acct_wrapper and rst_wrapper do not accept or respond to jtag_unlock, meaning the access-control table and reset-controller are not reset in synchrony, leading to an inconsistent security state after a JTAG unlock event.", "security_impact": "1) Asserting jtag_unlock clears all reglk_mem values to 0, disabling all software-visible register locks for all peripherals. 2) The ACCT and RST_CTRL peripherals retain their prior state, which may allow or deny accesses inconsistently with the now-cleared lock state. Combined, a JTAG attacker can unlock all software-enforced register lock protections with a single signal assertion.", "confidence": "high", "uncertainty_or_missing_evidence": "The JTAG unlock authentication mechanism (fuse-based hash comparison in dmi_jtag) is not fully visible in this scope, so the ease of triggering jtag_unlock from an untrusted context is not fully assessable.", "recommended_follow_up": ["Review the security requirements for JTAG-triggered reset of lock registers.", "If JTAG unlock should also reset ACCT and RST_CTRL, add jtag_unlock to their reset conditions.", "If JTAG unlock should NOT clear lock registers, remove jtag_unlock from the reglk_wrapper reset condition.", "Ensure consistent security-state reset across all security-critical modules on any unlock event."]}, {"finding_id": "FIND-06", "status": "confirmed_finding", "summary": "Boot ROM mux always selects rom_rdata_linux regardless of ariane_boot_sel_i, effectively ignoring the baremetal boot option", "vulnerability_category": "Incorrect Boot Mode Selection / Logic Error", "affected_locations": [{"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/openpiton/riscv_peripherals.sv", "line_start": 534, "line_end": 534, "module": "riscv_peripherals", "signal_or_register": "rom_rdata"}], "evidence": [{"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/openpiton/riscv_peripherals.sv", "line_start": 534, "line_end": 534, "module": "", "object": "rom_rdata mux assignment", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "`assign rom_rdata = (ariane_boot_sel_i) ? rom_rdata_linux : rom_rdata_linux;` — both branches of the ternary return rom_rdata_linux.", "supports_claim": "The baremetal bootrom output rom_rdata_bm is never selected. The comment above even acknowledges the intended behavior: `// we want to run in baremetal mode when using pitonstream`."}, {"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/openpiton/riscv_peripherals.sv", "line_start": 519, "line_end": 524, "module": "", "object": "bootrom_bm instantiation", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "`bootrom i_bootrom_bm (.rdata_o(rom_rdata_bm))` — baremetal bootrom is instantiated and its output connected, but the output is never used in the mux.", "supports_claim": "Dead instantiation confirms the bug: the baremetal ROM exists but its data is discarded."}], "reasoning_summary": "The boot ROM selection mux has both ternary branches pointing to `rom_rdata_linux`. The baremetal bootrom (`rom_rdata_bm`) is instantiated but its output is unreachable. This means the system always boots Linux regardless of the boot-selection input. While this may not directly violate a permission model, it represents a security-relevant logic fault: if the Linux boot image is less secure than a baremetal alternative (e.g., contains more attack surface), the inability to select a secure baremetal boot mode reduces overall system security posture.", "security_impact": "The system is permanently forced to boot Linux regardless of ariane_boot_sel_i. If the intended secure boot path is baremetal (e.g., for initial key provisioning or secure enclave operation), this fault permanently bypasses it. It may also indicate broader code integrity issues in this file.", "confidence": "high", "uncertainty_or_missing_evidence": "The security implications depend on the content of the two bootroms; rom_rdata_bm and rom_rdata_linux sources are not in scope.", "recommended_follow_up": ["Fix the mux: `assign rom_rdata = (ariane_boot_sel_i) ? rom_rdata_linux : rom_rdata_bm;`", "Audit whether ariane_boot_sel_i is used correctly in other parts of the design."]}], "no_finding_reason": "N/A — multiple findings were identified.", "global_uncertainty": "1. The full register-lock bit-field architecture specification is not available in the input scope; some lock-bit index anomalies are assessed by comparing sibling entries rather than against a formal spec. 2. The JTAG authentication logic (dmi_jtag, fuse_mem) is not fully in scope, so the precise threat model for jtag_unlock is partially inferred. 3. Peripheral index-to-register mapping for acct_mem beyond what is directly visible in rst_wrapper is inferred from context. 4. Some wrapper modules referenced in riscv_peripherals (aes0_wrapper, hmac_wrapper, sha256_wrapper, pkt_wrapper, etc.) are not in the input scope, so their individual register-lock implementations cannot be audited."}
